objections.description: The artifact is truncated mid-CSS-rule inside the prefers-reduced-motion media query. The entire motion system definition (Section 4.1) is incomplete — reduced motion overrides for all duration tokens are missing, the @keyframes definitions referenced by the motion system are absent, the will-change management strategy described in prose has no implementation, and Sections 4.2+ (presumably keyframes, stagger utilities, transition classes) are entirely missing. This is not a minor omission: the convergence criteria explicitly require motion behavior approval, and no agent can approve a motion system that doesn't exist past its token declarations.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete Section 4.1 reduced-motion overrides for ALL duration tokens (not just --duration-fast), then deliver the full remaining sections: @keyframes for screen transitions (slide-in-from-right, slide-out-to-left, fade-in, fade-out), action sheet entrance/exit, list stagger animations, and the will-change lifecycle management (apply on animation-start, remove on animationend). Include the CSS classes that compose these keyframes with the timing tokens.

objections.description: The spec stops at Section 4.1. The deliverables require: component architecture map (Section 5?), core layout shell, at least 3 key screens (Dashboard/Home, Detail/Timeline, Action sheet/Form), dark/light theme switching hook implementation, the useNavigationDirection hook referenced in Section 1, and the API client mentioned in the file tree. None of these are present. Without component implementations, this is an infrastructure spec, not an implementation spec — the convergence loop cannot approve 'component structure' or 'visual hierarchy' on components that don't exist.
objections.suggested_fix: Deliver the remaining sections: (5) Component architecture map with props/responsibilities for Shell, Header, SearchScreen, DocumentScreen, ActionSheet, and all shared primitives. (6) Core layout shell implementation (AppShell, responsive sidebar/nav). (7) Three screen implementations with full JSX, Tailwind classes referencing the token system, and dark mode coverage. (8) Theme switching hook (useTheme) with localStorage persistence matching the FOUC prevention script. (9) API client module with error handling, abort controllers, and loading states. (10) Test strategy with at least skeleton test files for critical paths.

objections.description: The code snippet defines ImmutableAssetsCacheMiddleware, references `app.get('/healthz')` and `app.exception_handler`, but never shows `app = FastAPI()` or how this integrates with the EXISTING web_server.py. The instruction says 'minimal, surgical changes' but presents a standalone snippet. Without showing the actual diff against the existing file, whoever implements this will either duplicate the app instantiation or break existing route registration. The `validate_frontend_build()` function is defined but never called — there's no `@app.on_event('startup')` or lifespan handler invoking it.
objections.suggested_fix: Show this as a diff against the existing web_server.py structure. Wire validate_frontend_build() into the FastAPI lifespan or startup event. Explicitly mark which lines are NEW vs which are existing-and-unchanged. Example: use FastAPI's lifespan context manager pattern: `@asynccontextmanager async def lifespan(app): validate_frontend_build(); yield` and pass to `FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)`.

objections.description: The config uses top-level `await` (`await import('rollup-plugin-visualizer')`) inside `defineConfig()` which is a synchronous function. This will throw a syntax error unless the config file is treated as an async module, but Vite's config resolution only supports top-level await if the function itself is async. The `defineConfig` call needs to be wrapped in an async factory, or the dynamic import needs to be restructured.
objections.suggested_fix: Use Vite's async config pattern: `export default defineConfig(async () => { const visualizer = analyzeMode ? (await import('rollup-plugin-visualizer')).visualizer : null; return { plugins: [react(...), visualizer?.({ ... })].filter(Boolean), ... }; });`

objections.description: The script uses `import.meta.dirname` which is only available in Node.js >=21.2 (or >=20.11 with the flag). If CI runs Node 18 or 20 LTS (both common), this script crashes. Also, `gzip -c ... | wc -c` won't work on Windows CI runners. No Node version requirement is specified anywhere in the spec.
objections.suggested_fix: Use `import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'; const __dirname = fileURLToPath(new URL('.', import.meta.url));` for broader Node compatibility. Replace the shell gzip call with Node's built-in `zlib.gzipSync` to avoid platform dependency. Add `engines: { 'node': '>=18' }` to package.json.

objections.description: The contrast ratios appear manually computed and several look incorrect. For example, #A1A1A6 on #0A0A0B: relative luminance of #A1A1A6 ≈ 0.363, #0A0A0B ≈ 0.0033, ratio ≈ (0.363+0.05)/(0.0033+0.05) ≈ 7.75:1 — close to the stated 7.2:1 but not exact. More critically, #8E8E93 on #242428 is stated as 3.8:1 AA-large with a ⚠️ but the spec then says in the usage rules 'NOT used on bg-elevated (dark)' — yet there's no lint rule, Tailwind config, or runtime enforcement mechanism. This is a code-review-only guardrail on a contrast violation, which means it WILL ship eventually.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a `tailwind.config.js` safelist/blocklist approach or a custom ESLint rule that flags `text-tertiary` usage inside `bg-elevated` containers. At minimum, add an axe-core integration test that catches contrast violations at test time. The contrast matrix should be regenerated programmatically (e.g., a script that takes hex values and outputs the table) rather than hand-verified.

suggestions.description: The spec references a `useNavigationDirection` hook (Section 5.6) that doesn't exist yet. Since this hook drives animation direction selection, its implementation should be part of the motion system section rather than deferred to an unwritten section. Include the hook implementation alongside the animation keyframes so they can be reviewed together.

Phase 1 parallel deploy is well-designed, but there's no mention of how to handle API responses that return URLs (e.g., if any API returns links to documents). If the API returns `/document/abc123`, that works for both old and new frontends. But if there's any server-rendered redirect or Location header logic, it needs to be base-path-aware during Phase 1. Worth an explicit 'API contract: all URLs are relative, no absolute paths in responses' statement.

The inline script tries localStorage then sessionStorage, but the useTheme hook (not yet written) presumably writes to localStorage. If localStorage is unavailable (private Safari), the script falls back to sessionStorage, but the React hook won't know to also write to sessionStorage. Document the contract: the hook must mirror the fallback chain (try localStorage, catch → sessionStorage, catch → memory-only).

Good discipline. Consider adding the policy for when a dependency request DOES come in: who approves, what's the review process, is there a max-size threshold for auto-reject. A one-liner like 'Any new runtime dependency requires a PR with bundle-size-before/after from build:analyze and approval from 2 agents' would formalize this.

The z-index `--z-dropdown-inside-modal: 450` is a good pragmatic addition, but consider also defining `--z-action-sheet` explicitly since ActionSheet is a first-class screen component rendered as an overlay. It likely needs `--z-overlay` or `--z-modal` but being explicit prevents future ambiguity.
